title: Why cannot you join with me in condemning Origen, and so put an end to our quarrel?

source.title: NPNF2-03. Theodoret, Jerome, Gennadius, & Rufinus: Historical Writings

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I call Jesus the Mediator to witness that it is against my will, and fighting against necessity, that I come down into the arena of this war of words, and that, had you not challenged me, I would have never broken silence.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Even now, let your charges against me cease, and my defence will cease.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: For it is no edifying spectacle that is presented to our readers, that of two old men engaging in a gladiatorial conflict on account of a heretic; especially when both of them wish to be thought catholics.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Let us leave off all favouring of heretics, and there will be no dispute between us.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: We once were zealous in our praise of Origen; let us be equally zealous in condemning him now that he is condemned by the whole world.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Let us join hands and hearts, and march with a ready step behind the two trophy-bearers of the East and West.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: We went wrong in our youth, let us mend our ways in our age.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If you are my brother, be glad that I have seen my errors; if I am your friend, I must give you joy on your conversion.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: So long as we maintain our strife, we shall be thought to hold the right faith not willingly but of necessity.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Our enmity prevents our affording the spectacle of a true repentance.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If our faith is one, if we both of us accept and reject the same things, (and it is from this, as even Catiline testifies, that firm friendships arise), if we are alike in our hatred of heretics, and equally condemn our former mistakes, why should we set out to battle against each other, when we have the same objects both of attack and defence?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Pardon me for having praised Origen’s zeal for Scriptural learning in my youthful days before I fully knew his heresies; and I will grant you forgiveness for having written an Apology for his works when your head was grey.
